label: Video topic
placeholder: Explain what the video should be about.
helper: Describe the subject, product, story, question, or idea the video should cover.
why: The topic is the central factual and creative input that cannot be safely inferred.

label: Primary goal
placeholder: Select the main result you want.
helper: Choose the single most important purpose of the video.
why: The goal determines the video structure, emphasis, CTA, and pacing.

label: Target audience
placeholder: Who should watch this, and what do they already know?
helper: Describe the intended viewer, relevant interests, and awareness level.
why: Audience knowledge and motivation materially affect language, examples, and hook selection.

label: Main takeaway
placeholder: What should viewers understand, remember, or believe?
helper: State the one central message the video must communicate.
why: A defined takeaway prevents the short script from becoming unfocused.

label: Facts to include
placeholder: Add the facts, steps, features, dates, examples, or details that must appear.
helper: Include only information that is accurate and approved for use.
why: The model must receive the factual details it is not permitted to invent.

label: Output language
placeholder: Select the script language.
helper: The script, on-screen text, caption, and notes will use this language.
why: Language cannot always be reliably inferred from the topic or target platform.

label: Target platform
placeholder: Choose where the video will be published.
helper: Select one platform or use a broadly compatible vertical-video format.
why: The destination affects pacing, tone, CTA language, caption style, and publishing conventions.

label: Target duration
placeholder: Choose the approximate duration.
helper: The spoken word count and number of scenes will be adapted to this duration.
why: Duration directly limits the amount of spoken content and the number of visual beats.

label: Video format
placeholder: Select a format or let the model decide.
helper: Choose the presentation structure that best fits your idea.
why: Different video formats require different narrative and scene structures.

label: Viewer action
placeholder: Choose what viewers should do next.
helper: Use one clear action or select no CTA for a purely informational ending.
why: The desired action determines how the final beat should conclude.

label: Offer details
placeholder: Describe the product, service, price, offer, destination, and approved claims.
helper: Include only confirmed information that may appear in the promotional script.
why: Commercial scripts require accurate offer information that must not be fabricated.
display: Show when primaryGoal is “Promote or sell”.

label: Platform name
placeholder: Enter the platform or publishing destination.
helper: Add the platform name and any important format requirement you know.
why: A custom destination may have conventions that differ from the supported platform presets.

label: Delivery method
placeholder: How will the message be presented?
helper: The script and visual directions will match the selected recording style.
why: Spoken delivery and scene instructions differ substantially between recording methods.

label: Available visuals
placeholder: Select assets you can realistically use.
helper: The script will avoid requesting footage or assets that are unavailable.
why: Visual directions must remain practical for the creator’s available assets.

label: Production level
placeholder: Choose the realistic editing level.
helper: Controls shot complexity, transitions, effects, and editing instructions.
why: The production level prevents unrealistic filming or editing recommendations.

label: Number of versions
helper: Generate one to three meaningfully different script versions.
why: The user may need alternative concepts without receiving an excessive number of versions.

label: Tone
placeholder: Select the desired tone.
helper: Choose the overall emotional and verbal style.
why: Tone materially changes word choice, hook intensity, delivery, and caption style.

label: Brand voice notes
placeholder: Add preferred wording, personality, phrases to avoid, or a short style sample.
helper: Leave blank to use a natural neutral voice suited to the audience.
why: Existing brand language cannot be inferred reliably from a general tone selection.

label: Pacing
placeholder: Choose the speed and rhythm.
helper: Controls sentence length, cut frequency, pauses, and information density.
why: Pacing affects both the spoken script and the editing rhythm.

label: Caption style
placeholder: Choose the accompanying post style.
helper: Controls caption length, CTA repetition, emoji use, and hashtag suggestions.
why: The publishing caption serves a different function from the spoken script and may require separate treatment.

label: Sources or references
placeholder: Paste approved notes, quotations, URLs, source excerpts, or product documentation.
helper: Use this for claims that must follow a specific source or approved wording.
why: Source material supports accurate claims and reduces the risk of invented information.

label: Restrictions
placeholder: List prohibited claims, sensitive details, required wording, or topics to avoid.
helper: Combine all special limitations and mandatory requirements here.
why: User-specific legal, brand, privacy, or factual restrictions cannot always be inferred.

label: Sensitive subject
placeholder: Select a regulated category when relevant.
helper: This enables additional caution for subjects where inaccurate claims may cause harm.
why: High-stakes subjects require stricter wording and verification safeguards.

label: Country or jurisdiction
placeholder: For example: Ukraine, United States, EU, California.
helper: Add the relevant location when laws, rights, benefits, or regulations may differ.
why: Legal, financial, political, employment, tax, insurance, and regulated claims may depend on jurisdiction.
display: Show when subjectRisk is not “General topic”.
